Article content: {"cover_media":"3_2033516765792395264","preview_text":"Why the future of dev agencies looks more like Kirkland & Ellis than a SaaS startup.
Every developer with an AI subscription and a GitHub account is now a one-person agency. They can ship in a weekend","media_entities":["3_2033557043647299585","3_2033548992437686272","3_2033520797176700928","3_2033547581046075392","3_2033525223975440384","3_2033522594163417088","3_2033551506327146496"],"title":"The law firm model for software developers and agencies","entities":{"urls":[{"text":"https://www.datacamp.com/resources/webinars/progress-from-junior-developer-to-10x-senior-ug"},{"text":"https://aroussi.com/post/from-junior-to-10x-dev"},{"text":"aroussi.com/subscribe"},{"text":"https://www.datacamp.com/resources/webinars/progress-from-junior-developer-to-10x-senior-ug"},{"text":"https://automaze.io"}]},"plain_text":"Why the future of dev agencies looks more like Kirkland & Ellis than a SaaS startup.
Every developer with an AI subscription and a GitHub account is now a one-person agency. They can ship in a weekend what used to take a team of four a month.
And yet, most of them are broke.
Not because their work is bad. Because they're spending half their time chasing leads, writing proposals, and sitting on sales calls instead of building. You can be 10x more productive and still starve if nobody knows you exist.
And if you're the client hiring that solo dev? You've got your own problems. They're expensive – architect-level talent doesn't come cheap. They have no backup – if they get sick or take a vacation, your project stops. They don't come with a designer, a DevOps engineer, or anyone to handle the marketing site for the product they just built. There's no firm behind them, no escalation path, no accountability beyond a handshake. You're betting your project on one person with no safety net.
So the developer can't survive alone. And the client can't rely on them alone either.
So they join agencies. And the moment they do, they're employees again. Their 10x leverage gets absorbed into someone else's margin. They trade autonomy for stability – which is exactly the bargain they were trying to escape.
This is the trap. And I think the way out has been sitting in plain sight for centuries.
Law firms figured it out 200 Years Ago
A great lawyer has the same tension as a great developer: they want autonomy and ownership, but they also need deal flow, brand credibility, and operational support. Things that are nearly impossible to build alone, while also doing the actual work.
The partnership model threads the needle.
Partners own their practice and earn from their own billings – but they operate under a shared brand, with shared infrastructure and a shared profit pool. The firm's success lifts every partner.
Associates aren't cheap labor. They're the pipeline. Today's associate is tomorrow's partner. The firm invests in growing them because the model only works if you're continuously producing new partners.
I've spent 35 years building software and running teams. I run Automaze, a CTO-as-a-Service agency. And I believe this is exactly the model software agencies need to adopt.
The timing couldn't be better. Here's why.
 
The Architect: A partner by any other name
I've been writing about the evolution from junior developer to what I call the "10x senior." The endpoint of that progression is a role I call the Architect.
The Architect isn't just a senior developer. They're a hybrid: part product owner, part senior engineer, part team lead. They understand why something is being built, not just how. They own the architecture, the quality, and the client outcome.
They don't write much code anymore. 80-90% of their time goes to planning, architecture, and review. AI agents handle the implementation.
 
This is the natural evolution in a world where AI handles the typing and humans handle the thinking.
And here's the thing about someone who operates at this level: they're a hair short of being an entrepreneur. They have the skills to start their own thing. They understand the product. They can run a fleet of agents. They have opinions about how things should be built.
Which means, if you're running a traditional agency and your best architects are essentially entrepreneurs-in-waiting... you have a retention problem.
The only question is when they leave, not if.
Unless you give them a reason to stay.
 
My DataCamp talk on the topic:
https://www.datacamp.com/resources/webinars/progress-from-junior-developer-to-10x-senior-ug
 
Introducing... "The Pod"
Here's the structure I'm building toward – and the one I think will define the next generation of software agencies.
The fundamental unit is the pod. Think of it as a law firm partner's practice, reimagined for AI-augmented software delivery.
 
The pod has two essential halves: a front and a back.
The Delivery Manager is the client's point of contact. Communication, expectations, scoping, relationship management. In a law firm, this is the partner who takes the client calls and translates between business needs and legal strategy. The Delivery Manager does the same – translating between what the client wants and what the Architect builds.
The Architect is the entire back end. Technical decisions, architecture, agent workflows, quality. They don't talk to clients unless there's a technical escalation. Their job is to think, plan, direct agents, review output, and ship.
This separation is critical. The Architect's leverage comes from uninterrupted focus on delivery, not from sitting in status calls.
The juniors aren't there for their coding skills. They're there to learn – to absorb the Architect's judgment, to understand how to manage AI agents, to build the mental models that turn a coder into a thinker. Law firm associates learning to become partners.
The AI agents do the heavy lifting: scaffolding, implementation, testing, documentation, migrations, bug fixes. The Architect directs them the way a senior partner directs associates – with clear specifications, quality standards, and review checkpoints.
In larger or more autonomous pods, a Pipeline Person can be added – a dedicated sales and business development lead with their own calendar and outreach. This turns the pod into a nearly self-sufficient unit, closer to a law firm partner who brings in their own clients.
What the firm provides
Multiple pods operate under a shared firm. Not a holding company skimming off the top. The operational blanket that makes the pod model possible – the same way a law firm's infrastructure lets partners focus on practicing law instead of running a business.
Brand and reputation – clients trust the firm, which opens doors for every pod
Sales and client acquisition – centralized pipeline so Architects can focus on building
Shared specialists – designers, DevOps, infrastructure teams across pods
Marketing and content – copywriting, landing pages, product launch support – the non-engineering work every project needs but no single pod should staff for
AI infrastructure – standardized agent platforms, tooling, best practices
Servers and hosting – shared compute, deployment pipelines, operational infrastructure
Operational overhead – contracts, billing, legal, HR
The shared profit pool – a portion of every pod's revenue flows into a common pot, distributed across all pod owners
No individual pod needs a full-time designer, a dedicated DevOps engineer, or a marketing team. But every pod occasionally needs all of them – especially when launching a product, building a client-facing landing page, or writing the copy that sells what they've built.
Software delivery doesn't end at the code. The firm provides these as shared services – the same way a law firm's research librarians, IT department, and marketing team serve all partners.
 
The economics: Profitability over income
Here's the part most people get wrong.
The goal is not to pay pod owners more than they'd earn going solo. That's a bidding war you'll eventually lose.
The goal is to make staying so much easier that the slightly higher income of going solo isn't worth the hassle.
Could a top Architect go independent and earn a bit more per project? Probably. But they'd spend half their week on sales, contracts, invoicing, infrastructure, and marketing. Work that has nothing to do with building software.
The math that matters isn't raw income. It's the ratio of profitability to overhead.
A pod owner who earns slightly less per dollar of revenue but spends 90% of their time on actual delivery is more profitable per hour of their life than a solo operator who earns more but burns half their week on everything else.
Easier at comparable income beats harder at slightly higher income. Every time.
 
In a traditional agency, this bargain doesn't exist. Senior devs earn a salary. The agency captures the delta between what the client pays and what the developer costs. As AI makes developers more productive, that delta grows – but compensation doesn't. The incentive to leave increases with every productivity gain.
The law firm model flips this. Pod owners earn a share of the revenue their pod generates. Skin in the game. Better delivery, more revenue, direct upside.
The shared pool keeps everyone aligned with the firm's success, not just their own pod's. When one pod builds something that elevates the firm's reputation, everyone benefits. This is what prevents the firm from becoming a loose collection of freelancers sharing a logo.
And software agencies have a structural advantage over law firms: dramatically lower overhead. No office leases, no paralegal armies, no malpractice insurance. The revenue split can be significantly more favorable to the pod owner than what a law firm associate gets.
Internal Pods: Your McKinsey Quarterly
Not every pod runs client work.
Some of the most valuable pods work on internal projects: SaaS products, educational content, open-source tools, research.
These pods need budget before they generate revenue. But they serve a critical function: building the firm's brand and intellectual property.
McKinsey publishes the McKinsey Quarterly not because it's a profit center, but because it makes every partner more credible in client meetings. Bain's research opens doors. BCG's frameworks become industry language.
An agency's internal pods work the same way. An open-source project with millions of downloads. A newsletter with tens of thousands of subscribers. A SaaS product that showcases technical capabilities.
These aren't distractions. They're the engine that drives inbound client interest for every pod in the firm.
The shared profit pool funds these internal pods. The brand value they create flows back to every pod owner through better deal flow and higher-value clients.
 
The Franchise play
Here's where it gets really interesting.
H&R Block didn't become massive by being one great accounting firm. They created a system – brand, process, technology, training – that let thousands of individual operators plug in and deliver consistent results.
The same model could work for software delivery.
Imagine pod owners across different cities, countries, and time zones – all operating under a shared brand, using shared AI infrastructure, with shared sales and marketing. Each pod independent in delivery. The system around them providing deal flow, tooling, operational support, and quality standards.
This isn't a franchise where you pay for a logo and a manual. The franchise value is the entire operational blanket: brand, sales engine, AI infrastructure, shared specialists. Every pod running on the same agent platform, backed by the same brand, fed by the same deal flow.
The big four consulting firms employ over a million people combined. The top law firms have thousands of partners. The software services industry is larger than both – and has no equivalent structure.
Agencies today are tiny shops of 2-10, mid-size firms of 50-200 trying to act big, or massive outsourcing operations in low-cost regions.
The pod model creates a new category.
A distributed network of high-leverage architects, each running their own AI-augmented pod, connected by shared infrastructure, brand, and economics.
Small enough to be fast. Connected enough to be credible. Aligned enough to compound.
 
 
Growing architects, not hiring them
None of this works without a continuous supply of Architects. And you can't hire Architects. You have to grow them.
The junior developer pipeline becomes strategic, not charitable. Juniors in this model aren't writing boilerplate. They're learning to think architecturally, manage AI agents, and own product outcomes.
The progression:
Foundation. Learn by watching the Architect. Review agent output. Understand why decisions are made, not just what.
Assisted. Take ownership of smaller workstreams. Direct agents on defined tasks. Build the judgment muscle.
Full Leverage. Run significant portions of delivery independently. The Architect reviews outcomes, not process.
Architect. Ready to spin up their own pod. New juniors cycle in beneath them.
 
That's dramatically faster than the traditional 3-5 year pipeline. AI compresses the teachable knowledge – syntax, patterns, frameworks. What can't be compressed is the earned knowledge – judgment, intuition, the ability to smell when something is wrong.
The pod accelerates earned knowledge by putting juniors directly alongside Architects making high-stakes decisions every day. Not in a classroom. Not in a bootcamp. In the room where it happens.
And when a junior is ready to graduate, they don't get handed an empty pod and told to go find clients.
Like law firms, where associates making partner are often endowed with a client they cultivated under their mentor, new Architects spin up a smaller "preview" pod – taking ownership of a client they were already managing. They've built the relationship. They know the codebase. The client trusts them.
A natural handoff, not a cold start. This is how you make the transition from associate to partner feel earned – not like a cliff jump.
My "From Junior to 10X Dev" article ↓
https://aroussi.com/post/from-junior-to-10x-dev
 
Why now?
Three things converging:
AI agents made individual developers dramatically more productive. A single Architect with a fleet of agents delivers what used to require a team of 5-8. The economics of the pod only work because of this shift.
Remote work is settled. A pod in London and a pod in Nashville operate under the same firm without anyone commuting. Prerequisite for the franchise model.
The talent market is bifurcating. Developers who learn to work with AI are becoming exponentially more valuable. Those who don't are becoming commoditized. The pod model attracts the first group with what no traditional employer offers: entrepreneurial upside with institutional support.
 
The era of the architect-led firm
To the Architect: you're good enough to go solo, but you don't want to spend half your life on sales calls. Join a firm that gives you a pod, a brand, deal flow, and AI infrastructure. You own your delivery. You share in the revenue. You build something with your name on it – inside a structure that lets you focus on what you're actually good at.
To the client: you're not hiring a faceless agency. You're getting a dedicated delivery manager who knows your business and a technical Architect who owns your project the way a law firm partner owns your case. Both have skin in the game.
To the industry: the era of the traditional dev shop is ending. The era of the architect-led, AI-augmented, partnership-structured software firm is beginning.
It's an old model applied to new technology. And it's going to eat the agencies that don't adapt.
This is the model I'm building. The exact revenue splits, partnership tiers, and governance structure are still being worked out – I'll share them as I do.
But the thesis is clear: law firms figured out how to retain entrepreneurial talent two centuries ago. It's time the software industry caught up.

If you liked this post, consider signing up for my newsletter @ aroussi.com/subscribe"}
